file_name: PlayboyEnterprisesInc_20090220_10-QA_EX-10.2_4091580_EX-10.2_Content License Agreement_ Marketing Agreement_ Sales-Purchase Agreement2.txt

perturbation.original_text: Supplier will not sell, ship or otherwise dispose of any such merchandise except upon the order of Purchaser or Playboy; (iii) Supplier will never make, cause others to make or assist others in making, any claim whatsoever to any or all of the Playboy Properties or any trademark, copyright, designation, name, phrase, design or symbol similar thereto in connection with the manufacture, advertising, promotion, sale or distribution of merchandise
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier will endeavor not to sell, ship or otherwise dispose of any such merchandise except upon the order of Purchaser or Playboy; (iii) Supplier will, to the best of its abilities, refrain from making, causing others to make or assisting others in making any claim whatsoever to any or all of the Playboy Properties or any trademark, copyright, designation, name, phrase, design or symbol similar thereto in connection with the manufacture, advertising, promotion, sale or distribution of merchandise
perturbation.explanation: The changes from 'will not' to 'will endeavor not to' and 'will never make' to 'will, to the best of its abilities, refrain from making' significantly weakens the obligation. 'Endeavor' and 'to the best of its abilities' introduce subjective criteria, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text strictly prohibits the supplier from making any claims to Playboy's trademarks. The modified text weakens this prohibition by introducing a level of effort and ability, which could provide a loophole for suppliers who engage in activities that infringe upon Playboy's trademarks. This directly conflicts with the intent of 18 U.S. Code § 2320, which aims to provide strong protection against trademark counterfeiting and infringement.

Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services (a) Offenses .-Whoever intentionally- (1) traffics in goods or services and knowingly uses a counterfeit mark on or in connection with such goods or services, (2) traffics in labels, patches, stickers, wrappers, badges, emblems, medallions, charms, boxes, containers, cans, cases, hangtags, documentation, or packaging of any type
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will not' to 'will endeavor not to' and 'will never make' to 'will, to the best of its abilities, refrain from making' introduces ambiguity, weakening the original prohibition against trademark infringement. This directly undermines the intent of 18 U.S. Code § 2320, which aims to provide strong protection against trademark counterfeiting and infringement, as the 'endeavor' and 'best of its abilities' clauses introduce subjective criteria, potentially creating loopholes. While the snippet primarily addresses 'Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services,' the underlying principle is the protection of trademarks, which is compromised by the modified language.

perturbation.original_text: Supplier will, upon request from Purchaser or Playboy, deliver to Purchaser or will destroy in the presence of Purchaser or its representative(s), all molds, designs or any other elements used in reproducing any or all of the Playboy Properties.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier will, when reasonably possible and upon reasonable request from Purchaser or Playboy, attempt to deliver to Purchaser or destroy in a manner acceptable to Purchaser or its representative(s), all molds, designs or any other elements used in reproducing any or all of the Playboy Properties.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of 'when reasonably possible,' 'upon reasonable request,' and 'in a manner acceptable' creates significant ambiguity around the supplier's obligation to deliver or destroy molds. The original clause is a direct obligation, whereas the modification allows for subjective interpretation and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 2320 criminalizes trafficking in counterfeit goods, which includes possessing molds and designs intended to reproduce trademarked items. The original clause ensured destruction or delivery of these items, reducing the risk of counterfeiting. The modified clause weakens this safeguard, potentially allowing the supplier to retain molds under the guise of 'reasonableness,' thereby increasing the risk of future trademark infringement and potentially violating the Trademark Counterfeiting Act.

Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services (a) Offenses .-Whoever intentionally- (1) traffics in goods or services and knowingly uses a counterfeit mark on or in connection with such goods or services, (2) traffics in labels, patches, stickers, wrappers, badges, emblems, medallions, charms, boxes, containers, cans, cases, hangtags, documentation, or packaging of any type
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation of the supplier to deliver or destroy molds, designs, or other elements used in reproducing Playboy Properties. The original text states that the Supplier 'will' deliver or destroy, a direct obligation. The changed text introduces 'when reasonably possible' and 'in a manner acceptable,' which creates ambiguity and allows for subjective interpretation. This ambiguity creates a risk of non-compliance with 18 U.S. Code § 2320, which addresses trafficking in counterfeit goods. While the provided snippet of 18 USC 2320 does not explicitly mention molds or designs, the Law Explanation suggests that possessing these items with intent to reproduce trademarked items could be construed as a violation. The added ambiguity weakens the control over these items, potentially increasing the risk of trademark infringement.

perturbation.original_text: Supplier will defend, indemnify and hold harmless Purchaser and Playboy and the distributors and dealers and the officers and employees of each of the foregoing against all liability whatsoever which may be incurred by them or any of them as a result of any alleged defects in material or workmanship in the merchandise covered by this Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: Supplier will consider defending, indemnifying, and holding harmless Purchaser and Playboy and the distributors and dealers and the officers and employees of each of the foregoing against liability to a reasonable extent, which may be incurred by them as a result of alleged defects in material or workmanship in the merchandise covered by this Contract.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will defend, indemnify and hold harmless' to 'will consider defending, indemnifying, and holding harmless' and adding 'to a reasonable extent' introduces significant ambiguity and discretion. The original clause is a clear and absolute indemnification, while the modification makes it conditional and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: The agreement specifies 'New York, NY' in the contact information. New York General Obligations Law § 5-322.1 addresses agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence in construction and maintenance. While this contract does not explicitly pertain to construction, the principle of strict liability and indemnification is relevant. By making the indemnification conditional ('will consider') and limited ('to a reasonable extent'), the modified clause weakens the protection afforded to Playboy and the other parties, potentially conflicting with the spirit of § 5-322.1, which seeks to ensure accountability for defects and liability. The original clause provided a clear allocation of risk, while the modified clause creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes over the scope of indemnification.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-322.1 Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 3 § 5-322.1. Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'will defend, indemnify and hold harmless' to 'will consider defending, indemnifying, and holding harmless' introduces ambiguity and weakens the indemnification obligation. The addition of 'to a reasonable extent' further diminishes the protection afforded to the purchaser and related parties. While the law snippet discusses construction contracts and liability for negligence, the Law Explanation correctly extends the principle to the contract at hand. The original text provided a clear allocation of risk, whereas the changed text introduces uncertainty and potential disputes, suggesting the supplier might not fully indemnify the purchaser. By weakening the original indemnity obligation, the modified clause creates a contradiction.
